subject: Engineering
description: Initial project proposal is formulated. The primary goal is to develop a novel deep learning-based beamforming algorithm to improve spectral efficiency in multi-user MIMO wireless systems. A high-level plan is drafted, outlining literature review, scheme design, simulation, and analysis phases.
reason: This is the first event of the research project. A proposal is the necessary starting point to define the core research questions, establish clear goals, and create an initial roadmap before any technical design or experimentation begins.

subject: Engineering
description: Based on the initial proposal, a detailed methodological scheme is developed. This involves selecting a specific deep neural network architecture (e.g., a convolutional neural network) to process channel state information and output optimal beamforming vectors. The simulation setup, including channel models and performance metrics (e.g., sum-rate), is also defined.
reason: Following the high-level proposal (e_01), the next logical step is to design the concrete technical method. This event translates the abstract `research_goals` into a specific `method_scheme`, which is a prerequisite for any implementation or pilot experiments.

subject: Engineering
description: A small-scale pilot simulation is conducted to validate the designed deep learning model. The experiment uses a simplified channel model with a small number of users to test the basic functionality of the beamforming algorithm and debug the implementation. The goal is to ensure the model can converge and produce meaningful output.
reason: After designing the initial `method_scheme` (e_02), a pilot experiment is the logical next step to provide a proof-of-concept. This small-scale test helps to verify the implementation's correctness and assess the fundamental viability of the proposed approach before committing resources to large-scale main experiments.

subject: Engineering
description: The results from the pilot simulation are analyzed. The analysis confirms the model converges, but the performance gain in terms of sum-rate is minimal compared to traditional baseline methods. This finding suggests the current neural network architecture or feature engineering is insufficient for achieving a significant improvement.
reason: After conducting a pilot experiment (e_03), the logical next step is to formally analyze the `experiment_results`. This analysis is crucial for evaluating the initial performance against the stated `research_goals` and will determine whether the current scheme is promising enough for main experiments or requires revision.

subject: Engineering
description: In response to the poor pilot results, the research team revises the methodological scheme. The original CNN is replaced with a Graph Neural Network (GNN) to better model the interference relationships between users. The input features are also enriched to include user location data, and the loss function is adjusted to better approximate the sum-rate maximization objective.
reason: The previous analysis (e_04) showed the initial method was insufficient. A logical and necessary next step in the research cycle is to return to the design stage to address the identified weaknesses. This event revises the `method_scheme` based on insights from the pilot `experiment_results`, demonstrating an iterative approach to problem-solving before committing to larger-scale experiments.

subject: Engineering
description: A second round of pilot experiments is conducted using the revised GNN-based scheme. The simulation is run under the same simplified conditions as the first pilot to allow for a direct comparison. The primary objective is to verify if the new GNN model can achieve a significant performance improvement over the previous CNN model and traditional baselines.
reason: After the previous method failed and was redesigned (e_05), it is crucial to validate the new approach. Running another pilot experiment is the most logical step to test the efficacy of the revised GNN-based `method_scheme` before committing the time and resources required for full-scale main experiments.

subject: Engineering
description: The results from the second pilot experiment are analyzed. The analysis reveals that the new GNN-based scheme significantly outperforms both the original CNN model and the traditional baseline methods in the simplified test environment. The sum-rate performance is promising, suggesting the GNN approach is viable for larger-scale evaluation.
reason: After conducting a second pilot experiment (e_06) with a revised method, the immediate next step is to analyze the results. This analysis is crucial to validate whether the new GNN-based `method_scheme` has successfully overcome the limitations of the previous approach and is promising enough to justify proceeding to the main experiments.

subject: Engineering
description: Buoyed by the promising pilot results, the team proceeds with the main experiments. The simulation environment is scaled up to include a larger number of users and antennas, more complex channel models, and a comprehensive comparison against several state-of-the-art baseline algorithms. The goal is to collect robust data on the GNN-based scheme's performance and generalizability.
reason: The successful analysis of the second pilot study (e_07) validated the GNN-based `method_scheme`. Proceeding to `main_experiments` is the logical next step to rigorously test the algorithm's performance and generalizability under realistic conditions, moving the project from proof-of-concept to a full-fledged evaluation.

subject: Engineering
description: A comprehensive analysis of the main experiment results is performed. The data confirms that the GNN-based beamforming scheme consistently outperforms all baseline methods, achieving a 15-20% increase in sum-rate across various user loads and channel conditions. The analysis also highlights the model's computational efficiency, making it a practical solution.
reason: Following the execution of main experiments (e_08), a detailed analysis of the collected `experiment_results` is the critical next step. This process validates the performance of the `method_scheme` against the project's `research_goals` and distills the raw data into key findings and conclusions that will form the core of the research paper.

subject: Engineering
description: With strong results from the main experiments, the team begins drafting the research paper. The initial focus is on the core technical sections: the 'Methodology' section describing the GNN-based scheme and the 'Results' section presenting the performance comparisons and analysis.
reason: The comprehensive analysis of the main experiments (e_09) has yielded conclusive positive results, marking the completion of the core research phase. The logical next step is to begin disseminating these findings. This 'writing' event translates the validated `method_scheme` and the compelling `experiment_results` into the first draft of the paper, moving the project towards publication.

subject: Engineering
description: While drafting the paper, the team realizes that a direct comparison of computational complexity is missing. A new set of experiments is conducted to measure the inference time and resource usage of the GNN model versus the baseline algorithms, providing crucial data on the practical viability of the solution.
reason: After starting the writing process (e_10), it is common to identify gaps in the evaluation. This event represents a realistic loop where the team returns to experimentation to gather missing data (computational complexity) prompted by the writing of the `paper_paragraph`, ensuring the final results are comprehensive enough to support the method's claims of practicality.

subject: Engineering
description: The results of the supplementary experiments on computational complexity are analyzed. The analysis shows that while the GNN model has a higher offline training cost, its online inference time is comparable to the iterative baseline algorithms, confirming its practical feasibility for real-time applications.
reason: After running specific experiments to measure computational complexity (e_11), the immediate next step is to analyze this data. This analysis provides the missing piece of information identified during the writing process (e_10) and is necessary before updating the `paper_paragraph` with the new findings on practical viability.

subject: Engineering
description: The team revises the 'Results' section of the paper to incorporate the new findings on computational complexity. A new subsection is added with tables and figures that compare the inference time of the GNN model against baselines, strengthening the argument for the method's practical applicability.
reason: After analyzing the supplementary experiments on computational complexity (e_12), the logical next step is to integrate these new `experiment_results` into the paper. This `writing` event updates the existing `paper_paragraph` to include the crucial data on practical feasibility, which was the reason for running the extra experiments in the first place (e_11).

subject: Engineering
description: The team focuses on completing the paper draft by writing the 'Introduction' and 'Conclusion' sections. The Introduction situates the work within the field, states the problem, and highlights the key contributions. The Conclusion summarizes the findings, reiterates the significance of the GNN-based approach, and suggests avenues for future research.
reason: With the core technical sections ('Methodology' and 'Results') now well-developed (e_13), the logical next step is to write the framing sections of the paper. Drafting the 'Introduction' and 'Conclusion' requires synthesizing the initial `research_goals` and the final `experiment_results` to create a compelling narrative for the work, moving the paper closer to a complete manuscript.

subject: Engineering
description: The team consolidates all drafted sections into a complete manuscript for internal review. This process involves polishing the text for clarity and flow, standardizing notation, and refining figures and tables to ensure a coherent and compelling presentation of the research.
reason: After drafting all the individual sections of the paper (e_10, e_13, e_14), the logical next step is to assemble them into a single, cohesive document. This internal review and refinement stage is crucial for improving the manuscript's quality and narrative strength before submitting it for publication or seeking external feedback.

subject: Engineering
description: Based on internal review feedback, the team performs a final revision of the manuscript. They refine the abstract, sharpen the claims in the introduction, and improve the visual presentation of the figures to make the paper more impactful for external reviewers.
reason: After consolidating the manuscript for internal review (e_15), the next logical step is to incorporate the feedback received. This final revision polishes the `paper_paragraph` based on the project's core `research_goals` and `experiment_results`, preparing the manuscript for submission and moving the project toward its conclusion.

subject: Engineering
description: With the manuscript fully revised and polished after internal feedback, the team submits the paper to a top-tier wireless communications conference. This marks the successful completion of the research project, having achieved the initial goals and produced a high-quality, impactful publication.
reason: The project has reached a natural conclusion. The research questions have been answered, the GNN-based method has been validated through extensive experiments, and a complete manuscript has been drafted and revised (e_16). Submitting the paper for publication is the final step, signifying the end of this research cycle.
